text: I'm curious about Tree Notation. How does it compare to something like, say, LLVM IR?
You could make a Tree Language that compiles to LLVM IR
Tree Notation is a dumb, error-less notation (like binary), that is just a slight step above binary, giving you trees, abstraction and scopes
Although dead simple, it has very interesting properties. It makes programs isomorphic to spreadsheets (instead of function arguments, for example, we have cells fixed in geometry)
It plays nicely with git -- all changes are semantic and nodes are line based
I could ramble on all day about it

But hopefully soon the tooling, demos, and docs will do a better job of explaining it than I can....(I've found it's actually often more helpful if someone else explains it to people, as I've been working on it for 7 years I forget what it's like to see it with fresh eyes)

If every tree is valid, and no syntax errors can happen, then what is to prevent a programmer from making logical mistakes? Isn't the whole progression of computer languages been one of a steady march towards having the computer do more of the detail work so as to reduce the chance for human error, which is the main thing we fight in computers. Machines are incredibly fast and have large storage now, the only thing limiting us is our ability to make reliable programs. Sorry, but the tree notation seems formless and thus not in the direction of progress. Change  my mind...

Hi <@UEQ6M68H0>. There are no errors at the Tree Notation level, just as there is no such thing as an error in binary notation. However, there are errors at the "Tree Language" level. If you try some of the sample languages in the designer app (you might need a stiff drink--the app is still early stages), you can make an error and see how because Tree Languages are so much easier to parse since the AST == Source Code and there's no visible syntax characters, we can create a whole new generation of more intelligent editing experiences. Right now, depending on the type of error, we are able to suggest autocorrections immediately. I 100% agree with you in the importance of reliability. I expect we see a reduction in software defects of 10x+ in Tree Projects vs current projects across the industry in a few years. Once you have put a software project in the minimal Tree form, it's done and timeless. The parts of our software stack that have the fewest bugs are all in Tree Languages now.

<@UMVFWPZ36> and Kartik, I’m very interested in what you learned about “how to research properly”, though it’s a tangent off a tangent now. I feel like that might be the most important thing I didn’t learn by not getting a PhD.

<@UD3AK9JRF> I don't have a phd either. I was able to get a job at UHCC because they needed people who knew cloud computing and machine learning. I didn't know biology but the team knows that (I've learned a lot but still far from an expert) and it's been a great partnership. I've worked alongside lots of bioinformaticians, researchers, and clinicians analyzing data and writing code ; been coauthor on 10 or so peer reviewed papers now (a few still in the review process) whereas before I couldn't define "peer review" ; I've been on the reviewer side of the table; have gone to the academic conferences and talks; gotten to know lots of academics and got some great mentors. So I think I at least now "know how to research properly" (thought I reserve my right to do it in a slightly different way :wink: ) which has been helpful. I also just doubled down heavily on getting better at building huge curated datasets, because data trumps brains :slightly_smiling_face:.
Thanks, Breck. What from that experience has been particularly useful in your FoC work? It also sounds like you’ve been involved in that academic work for a while, did you know Kartik beforehand?

Sounds like the first space on a line is special in Tree Notation? Is that accurate?

Well Breck, how about taking one or more of the examples from my github repository of the tiny 500 -1000 word programs, and show how they might be represented in your tree notation. In the case of the clock, the Beads notation form is already within spitting distance of the minimum possible length program, and i don't believe that merely putting things into AST form makes the program more understandable. The comments are gone in tree form as your examples in your paper show, so isn't this more like LISP without parentheses? I agree that editing in the AST has some advantages in terms of prediction, but creation of new content is more mysterious to me. This is why some people are heading towards fill-in-the-blanks type of approaches, so you have support for entering the fields you need, and the keywords are put in the right place. I think if you took a larger program like chess it might obscure the meaning of the code to put it into a deeply nested tree. Deep nesting is the enemy of comprehension, as LISP proved 50 years ago.

From <https://arxiv.org/pdf/1703.01192.pdf>:

> notations such as XML, JSON, and Racket can encode multilingual programs by complecting language blocks. For example, the JSON program below requires extra  nodes to encode Python:
> ```
> {"source": [
>   "import <http://hn.np|hn.np> as lz\n",
>   "print(\"aaronsw pdm ah as mo gb 28-3\")"
>   ]
> }
> ```
>
> With TN, the Python code requires no complecting:
> ```
> source
>  import <http://hn.np|hn.np> as lz
>  print("aaronsw pdm ah as mo gb 28-3")
> ```

It's not clear to me how the newline after `lz` does double-duty for both the newline inside the string and the comma (array separator) outside the string.
Edward's clock example is at the bottom of <https://github.com/magicmouse/beads-examples/blob/master/Example%201%20-%20Clock/Clock%20in%20Beads.md>

<@UF1ATC4RF> The first space on the line, if it is an increase of the indent level on the previous line, is referred to as the "X Increment (as in, it increments the x value for the location of the word on a 2D plane)....technically you can change XI to anything you want (along with YI and ZI), but space is the convention

<@UF1ATC4RF> The first space on the line, if it is an increase of the indent level on the previous line, is referred to as the "X Increment (as in, it increments the x value for the location of the word on a 2D plane)....technically you can change XI to anything you want (along with YI and ZI), but space is the convention

<@UEQ6M68H0> very interesting language! I like the clock example. I previously thought that we would have great general purpose Tree Languages by 2018, but the path to get there has turned out slightly different than I anticipated. basically, the tooling needs to advance ahead of the languages, otherwise the languages aren't a joy to use and the designers make subpar decisions on the language *because of the poor tooling*. So I would not tackle Beads head on today. We still need to keep tacking toward that direction. I bet in 2020 we'll start to see the first good general purpose languages (unless someone out there is quietly building a good one that I'm not aware of )

<@UEQ6M68H0> very interesting language! I like the clock example. I previously thought that we would have great general purpose Tree Languages by 2018, but the path to get there has turned out slightly different than I anticipated. basically, the tooling needs to advance ahead of the languages, otherwise the languages aren't a joy to use and the designers make subpar decisions on the language *because of the poor tooling*. So I would not tackle Beads head on today. We still need to keep tacking toward that direction. I bet in 2020 we'll start to see the first good general purpose languages (unless someone out there is quietly building a good one that I'm not aware of )

<@UEQ6M68H0> "Beads notation form is already within spitting distance of the minimum possible length program" it is very minimal on a "relative complexity" measure, but there is also the "absolute complexity" measure, for example, where is "draw_rect" defined?

<@UEQ6M68H0> " Deep nesting is the enemy of comprehension, as LISP proved 50 years ago." this is a really good point and something I have found in practice to be the case. That's why often I'm using more of a flat graph like structure in my Tree Languages and having the compiler or VM reconstruct the tree. However, I think this is because of limited tooling. It would have been impractical to build great tooling for deeply nested trees back in the day. Now with things like iPad pro, VR, AR, fast graphics cards, etc., I think in the next 10 years we might see the environment change to where that is not only a problem but a natural way to do things.
<@UCUSW7WVD> it does require the Tree Language to know to treat it's child as strings and concatenate its child nodes

<@UEQ6M68H0> "This is why some people are heading towards fill-in-the-blanks type of approaches" I love this. I'm going to try describing tree languages as "fill-in-the-blanks" type of languages and see if that makes more sense to people. Are there examples of this? IIRC I haven't seen this term before (other than "mad libs")

Is that part of the grammar the site mentions? I'm not clear on that side. And I think showing before and after without describing such a major knob risks overstating your conclusions.

the Eve language's early prototypes were clearly inspired by madlibs.  If i recall correctly Jonathan Edwards' subtext prototypes also used that concept, and then there are various shipping products that use the fill-in-the-blanks paradigm to the extent they can. In a way, intellitype suggestions are a kind of fill in the blanks.  Everyone knows that when you take a test, the easiest kind of test is true/false, followed by multiple choice, then fill in the blanks, then essay is the hardest. Since programming is mostly an essay test, it is the hardest kind of work, and moving it to fill in the blanks is taking it up one level of simplicity. That is unarguable. I haven't seen even a fake screen of an interface that moves i up to the highest two levels. So you ask for a chess program, it asks you a series of yes/no questions, and bingo you have a program.. ;->

<@UCUSW7WVD> I put some notes of the "Drawbacks" in the paper (notably: that Tree Notation is a lower level than something like JSON, and to get those higher level concepts you need to have a Tree Language(s)). I'm not worried about overstaying the conclusions. I still am always on the hunt for flaws, but after over 2 years and feedback from over 1k people without anyone providing a single proof by contradiction, and over 1k weekly users now, I'm slightly more confident in the grandiose claims? I dunno. Regardless, I'm more of an empiricist than a theorist, and think the best arguments in favor of Tree Notation will be killer apps. Which require killer tools. Which requires lots more work. :cry:
So Tree Notation encodes an ordered tree where each node has a string associated with it, right?

From the FAQ: "Tree Notation is the first notation where a visual design tool can generate code as good as someone can write by hand. Traditional languages have a critical flaw–there are infinite ways to represent any given structure. In Tree Notation there is only 1 way to represent 1 structure. This simplification is one of a few core reasons why Tree Notation is helping solve the Visual Programming problem."
I don't get this. Sure, there are many ways to encode the same program in PLs (e.g. single-line vs. multi-line arrays), but that's a feature and not a flaw. It allows programmers to insert whitespace such that the program becomes as readable as possible. Tree Notation seems disallow this. Sure, this means that it's easier for a VPL to generate human-grade code, but only because the human-grade code in Tree Notation is less readable than in current PLs. That's not an improvement for me.
<@UAL7940NM> " encodes an ordered tree where each node has a string associated with it, right?" yes

"only because the human-grade code in Tree Notation is less readable than in current PLs" I think the opposite will be true, in time. You can, btw, allow blank lines in your Tree Language. You just define a node type for that. I use this in my "Dumbdown" language (<http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20dumbdown>). Blank line nodes get turned into <br> tags.
Tree Notation just never ignores anything, or strips anything
Code in Tree Notation has a 3D representation:
the long term idea (10 - 20 years out) is to take this all the way to the hardware level
where you'd load your source code directly into the machine, which would have the same shape as your code
so a different computer model than a register based system
so lots of little details in tree notation, like not changing someone's source (by something like stripping whitespace) is made looking toward the future of where this could go

> Our theory is there is a natural notation sitting between binary notation and higher level languages that has been overlooked and it could be a silver bullet

Well put! I was thinking about this exactly, but in my head this thought developed into something different. I got this idea of storage for DAG (just like git), with generic browser that allows to edit and observe (just like text editors). This browser gonna look like Excel, but for trees (DAGs).

Maybe this is natural, simple foundation that is one step above binary?
<@UMVFWPZ36> I'd be interested in the 3D representation, can you provide some more info?
<@UAL7940NM>
a simple 3D vis of a program (made using Ohayo. shift+? to see the help and the 3D vis button)
with tree notation. programs are spreadsheets. spreadsheets are programs. so you get coordinates for each cell/word (well, we use a space instead of a tab, but you could really use either).
a talk I gave 2 years ago (there should be a video of the room somewhere, but I can't find it) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ldVtDlbOUMA>

text: early version of ohayo showing a 3D vis of a program

So the three dimensions are:
1. Lines,
2. Words per line,
3. Indentation
Right?
so the video especially with q&a is super helpful
some visualization notes: i think that it would be extremely helpful if you had some light sources and shading to help with distance
or even a strictly isometric view a la diablo or league of legends
it’s just tricky to pick up depth at the moment
also for visualization purposes seeing the axes labeled would be helpful, especially if they were labeled “depth,” “siblings,” and “contents” or something similarly descriptive

also for grokking it quicker it would be helpful if the orbs were labeled in some way or at least on tooltip to show the actual token (eg =fn) rather than with the nodes type to help make the relationship between source and visualization clearer
eg (warning not an artist and handwriting bad)
the other thing that’s helpful for me at least here is that a major way we perceive depth on a 2d screen is occlusion
so the spheres end up very far apart and don’t cover one another so it’s hard to tell where they are
another thing you might notice is that laying these dimensions out on a grid creates a false implied relationship
the parameter that fib takes isn’t strictly related to the call to fib with oneLess
but i quite like the visualization concept

if you could explain a step by step transformation of some js code into its tree language equivalent it would be super helpful. like it’s clear that you’re getting speed and clarity improvements but i can’t tell where they’re coming from
and i can’t parse how any of the tree notations work exactly

Awesome stuff, thanks <@UHWC9PXBL>! Your lego blocks vis is much better than the orbs and is the direction I think we should go in.... the new homepage tries to move toward that <https://treenotation.org/>
Created an issue to track this..... <https://github.com/treenotation/jtree/issues/73>
The 3D vis from 2017 used visjs, but for a 3D lego block style their might be a better one out there
This could be a great debugging tool

The "Explain" and "Explain Roots" buttons on the Designer app (<http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#>) show you the cell types and root cell types, respectively....we could Z-stack them into a vis which might be helpful
<@UAL7940NM> actually that talk I threw in the 3rd dimension to kind of see what people thought and see where it might go
I think it was a wrong direction, and we have since gone back to the simpler 2 dimensions
saving that 3rd dimension for type information extension stacks
and/or tree machines
So lines and columns are the 2 dimensions
YI = increment line
XI = increment cell/column/word

I was not able to understand the paper. It seemed like the comparison of { ”title”:”Web Stats”, ”visitors”:{”mozilla”:802} } to...

title Web Stats
  visitors
  mozilla 802

...was intended to be a full and complete specification of TN, but it really does not suffice at all.

A better representation would be a Haskell or Java/C# data structure that encodes TN and then show how arbitrary TN or arbitrary text maps to that data structure. Or, show how to convert arbitrary TN to JSON.

Hi <@UD6EXQVM0> , thanks for the feedback! You are right that example isn’t as clear as can be. Tree notation implements one data structure, a tree node, which has 2 members, one for content (binary/string), and an array of child trees..how the content is parsed is up to the higher level tree language. There is a more formal definition now in the GitHub/faq

I'm scratching my head about how this could be used in practice... given the intro in the paper, it follows that TN would encode these two JSON snippets the same way: {”mozilla”:802} and {”mozilla”:"802"}. And obvious questions arise like how you can store a string like `"   hello\n world\0"` in TN. My own LES language <http://loyc.net/les> answers these questions in a way that developers are already familiar with... and LES3 generalizes literals to support new data types. So what's the TN equivalent?
